data.title: Navitas: Overhyped Semiconductor Pure Play Coasting On Nvidia Hopes
data.description:
Thesis

Navitas Semiconductor Corporation (NASDAQ:NVTS) is a semiconductor company, which works with two main semiconductor materials, namely Gallium nitride [GaN] and silicon carbide [SiC], and is considered to be a primary player in Gallium Nitride [GaN] power ICs, and in the mobile adapter business.

Nonetheless, the company’s current revenues are mediocre in light of its current valuation, and revenue is going down quarter after quarter. Its current valuation appears to be primarily related to a statement of May 2025 related to an Nvidia (NVDA) collaboration. However, there is no certainty that Nvidia will actually choose Navitas for the development of such chips, among several other players.

A look at recent insider transactions reveals constant selling pressure from insiders, with no buying.

I do not see the termination of collaboration with (TSMC) and a partnership with Powerchip (PSMC) as a positive either.

I believe the company is far from being profitable in the near future. At a $1.48 billion valuation with neither revenues nor profitability to support that, the company looks heavily overvalued for the moment.

Navitas Semiconductor

Current business

Navitas considers itself the only pure-play, next-generation power-semiconductor company, with two main semiconductor materials namely GaN and SiC. This slide shows the size and efficiency gains of both semiconductors compared to traditional silicon.

GaN power ICs are claimed to enable up to 3x faster charging in half the size and weight of old, silicon-based power electronics. The advantage of gallium nitride are considered to be faster switching speeds, lower on-resistance and lower conduction losses, lower weight and lower cost.

GaN serves as basis for GaNFast integrated circuits as shown below.

SiC is used in GeneSiC power devices, which can be integrated in MOSFETs and diodes.

Both applications aim to improve speed, efficiency, reliability and cost-effectiveness, in a variety of markets, from mobile fast chargers to data centers to the EV industry.

The current business of Navitas revolves mostly around fast chargers for smartphones, laptops and tablets. Navitas claims that over 50 fast-charger products have adopted their GaNFast technology, and among the customers quoted are Lenovo, Xiaomi, Dell, and OPPO.

NVTS is not sitting still, and regularly announces new products or product developments. Some of the latest are:

- the inclusion of GaNSense Control ICs in Xiaomi’s 90W GaN chargers which should be the world’s smallest charger, a further development in its Xiaomi partnership;

- the launch of 12kW GaN & SiC platforms for AI data centers, as also presented at the company’s AI Tech Night;

- a collaboration with Taiwan’s Powerchip Semiconductor Manufacturing Corporation (PSMC) for the production of Navitas’ GaN portfolio as of the start of 2026.

Future plans

The plans of Navitas for future growth seem to move away from the above-mentioned low-margin business. The company’s orientation for the future would be more on high-end products with higher margins in businesses such as the AI data center market, solid-state transformers to convert high-voltage grid power to 800 volts, on-board DC-to-DC converters to convert 800 volts down to 48 volts, and processor power delivery to convert 48 volts to the low voltage which would actually be needed by the processor. This new focus would unlock a $2.6 billion per year market, in a growing market.

Due to AI implementation and the energy requirements of the most modern chips, this market is changing fast. Different to existing AC/DC powered data centers, the future AI data centers would require 800 volts HVDC, or high-voltage direct current, to be converted to lower voltage depending on the needs of the GPU’s. That would require changing many existing elements of the last elements of the grid up to the GPU’s.

Nvidia partnership and stock reaction

Bullish considerations

One press release deserves additional attention, because it provoked a massive stock move and revaluation of Navitas’s stock, as can be seen here.

To me it feels now that most of the current valuation is directly related to investor hopes that the partnership with Nvidia will lead to fruitful and profitable business for Navitas, so I want to dive into this a bit more.

On May 21, 2025, Navitas announced that its GaN and SiC technologies would be used for the development of Nvidia’s next-generation data center power infrastructure working at 800 volts HVDC, to support Nvidia’s Kyber rack-scale systems and Nvidia’s future Rubin Ultra chip.

Nvidia is redesigning data centers based on 800 volts HVDC, due to space constraints, lack of scalability, the need for a lot of copper, lack of energy efficiency, thermal challenges, risk of downtime and the need for various power supply units.

According to the last earnings call, Nvidia's Blackwells chips are using 4.5 kW power supplies, but the Blackwell Ultra and the upcoming Rubin will require 12 kW. Navitas has improved the design of its ICs, and is currently focusing on a new 12 kW platform.

There is no doubt that Navitas’ products offer some of the requirements needed for Nvidia’s vision on future data centers, as they are smaller, highly efficient, work fast and run cooler.

My bearish perspective

I want to place some caveats around this announcement. First of all, Navitas is not Nvidia’s manufacturer at this time. As shown in Nvidia’s blog, Nvidia is working with a series of companies to the same purpose. As for silicon providers, those are Analog Devices (ADI), Infineon (OTCQX:IFNNY) (OTCQX:IFNNF), Innoscience, MPS (MPWR), Navitas, OnSemi (ON), Renesas (OTCPK:RNECY), Rohm (OTCPK:ROHCF), STMicroelectronics (STM), and Texas Instruments (TXN). All of these are large multinational players with market caps in the tens of billions. For the sockets Navitas is competing for, the company’s main competitors are probably Infineon and Innoscience, as the others do not produce SiC-based products.

So to be correct, Navitas is competing with ten other companies to gain Nvidia’s favor, and among the companies quoted, Navitas is by all means not a preferred partner, but the smallest of all companies, which is unprofitable. In an already crowded space, the other companies may have more mature supply chains, better views on finances and profitability, better manufacturing and distribution options, etcetera. Given its current losses in a capital-intense environment, margin pressures and the lack of economies of scale may cause Navitas to have to outprice its competitors or succumb to margin pressure.

Furthermore, testing for the future development of products for Nvidia entails several stages, and we do not know at this stage which stage Navitas is in at present, nor whether it will be able to proceed to the next stage. We do know that volume production is planned for 2027, which is still a while out given Navitas’ cash burn.

We do not know what the margins of Navitas would be for this business.

I also believe that the fact that Navitas is no longer working with TSMC, which is by far the largest and considered best semiconductor company in the world, but now instead works with its Taiwanese competitor PSMC, may not play in its favor for Nvidia.

Financials

According to the latest quarterly results, Navitas had a total revenue of $14.5 million, and expects to have third quarter revenue of $10 million. For the first quarter, that was total revenue of $14 million. These compare to $20.5 and $23.2 million respectively for the same quarters last year, and $18 million for the last quarter of 2024. The year 2024 appeared to be a record revenue year, with $83 million in revenue [slide 5], and Navitas’ revenue streams over the years had been improving until then.

However, that growth trajectory seems stalled, as all of the 2025 numbers, whether for the past or the future, indicate that revenue appears to be going down considerably.

The company’s GAAP losses have also gone down over the above-mentioned quarters, going for example from $31 million in quarters 1 and 2 of 2024 to $25 and $21 million in the same quarters for 2025.

Navitas’ cash situation did improve over the last quarters, growing from $75.1 million as of March 31, 2025 to $161.2 million as of June 30, 2025, through among others the sale of 20 million common shares which generated $100 million.

This means Navitas should be good to keep its business going at least for the coming year without the need to raise additional money. However, it may do so nonetheless as it has over the past quarters.

A detailed discussion of the company’s latest numbers can be found in Seeking Alpha analyst’s Ahmed Abdelazim’s bullish coverage or Agar Capital’s bearish coverage.

I note at this time that revenue has been declining by almost 30% over the past year, and if guidance is true, that may be more in the vicinity of 50% when the results for the next quarter come in, as the company had reported $21 million in revenue for the third quarter of 2024. As explanations, the company has mentioned the strategic decision to deprioritize lower margin China mobile business, and a slowdown in SiC sales to China due to tariffs.

Valuation

At this time, if we were to add up all revenue for the four past quarters, we end up with $68.7 million in total revenue and $104.7 million in total losses.

The company’s current valuation is $1.53 billion, which represents 22 times its current revenue, while the business is not even profitable. Here are some further indicators for valuation purposes, which indicate unanimously that Navitas is valued extremely high when looking at its actual financial results.

Ongoing insider selling

What concerns me the most in all of this, apart from the large sales of common stock, is the constant insider selling that can be noticed since 2023 but particularly over the past months.

The below chart shows an overview of insider transactions over the past months, which indicates to me that a substantial amount of insiders have considerably reduced their positions in Navitas.

This is confirmed when looking at insider sales by the previous CEO Eugene Sheridan, who has recently been replaced.

Leadership change

Navitas announced in August 2025 that long-standing CEO and founder Gene Sheridan would no longer serve as the company’s CEO, and that Chris Allexandre would be nominated to take his place.

Risks to bear thesis

My view here is that a short position may be the best trade in Navitas for the reasons set out above. Short interest is currently at 22%.

Shorting always comes with risks, however, as losses are possibly unlimited.

It is not excluded that the stock may go higher as further news on the Nvidia collaboration and its progress may be shared with investors.

The copper crisis and tariffs of the Trump administration may also play in favor of the viability and profitability of Navitas’ own business model, as they are less dependent on copper.

The increased cash position also attracts investors, so even in a scenario where the Nvidia partnership would fall through at a given point, it is not excluded that the stock will keep on trading substantially higher than where it was prior to the Nvidia announcement.

Conclusion

I believe Navitas’ stock is overhyped at this time, mostly on the basis of an Nvidia announcement that does not guarantee success at this stage.

Navitas’ revenues do not paint a good picture of the ongoing business, the company keeps making losses, and as a result, keeps burning cash.

As the company would like to transition to other business than the one it is currently operating in, there is no certainty that these new businesses will find buyers at this time. If the company actually lands Nvidia as a new customer, then it remains to be seen by which time that would occur, for what volume, which margins can be used in such business, and whether it would eventually turn Navitas into a profit-making business, something it is very far from at this time.

The company’s seemingly never-ending insider sales paint a picture of many insiders trying to sell a substantial part of their positions, which does not give confidence to a bull thesis, rather to the contrary. The considerable issuance of common stock in the market over the past months is good to continue business, but also comes at the detriment of shareholders due to dilution.

I believe the company’s stock is currently heavily overvalued. While I do not exclude the possibility that further news on Nvidia may further boost the stock, I do not believe that at the moment, the fundamentals are there to support the high valuation the company has in light of its financial results.

For the reasons above, I believe a Strong Sell rating is appropriate here.

data.title: Starbucks: Value Has Suffered More Than Necessary; Give It A Break
data.description:
Over the years, Starbucks Corporation (NASDAQ:SBUX) has garnered global brand popularity and loyalty that have solidified its success and market positioning. However, it has faced several challenges since the pandemic, and until now, the scars are still visible. I perfectly understand negative analyst and investor sentiments amid rising cost pressures that continue to squeeze its margins.

Despite this, SBUX still appears resilient with its solid customer base and prudent marketing strategies. This is supported by its robust balance sheet that promises high sustainability and dividend longevity. With that, I think its value has already dropped more than necessary, which makes it cheap at this point. Technicals are still bearish but show some entry opportunities today.

Challenges

Starbucks Corporation has been through numerous challenges, like the pandemic that reduced foot traffic and high inflation in 2022 that weakened discretionary spending. In recent years, it has also had to deal with labor disputes and bargaining. Since 2021, nearly 650 SBUX stores with over 12,000 employees have been unionized. And until now, this remained a challenge after negotiations and bargaining had been stalled repeatedly. Workers continue to reject proposals largely due to the relatively high compensation for executives. As the issue continues, SBUX still potentially faces 'understaffing' according to the unionized SBUX stores.

Another challenge is stubborn global inflation that intensifies cost pressures and squeezes pricing flexibility and margins. In the US, inflation has recently picked up momentum once again to 2.9%. While this is already significantly lower than in previous years, positive inflation means the general price level is still increasing. This can be exacerbated by new tariffs due to its reliance on coffee bean imports from parts of Latin America and the Asia-Pacific.

Lastly, competition among coffee shops is more intense today. Starbucks not only competes with large brands like Tim Horton’s, Dunkin, and Peet’s Coffee but also with smaller local shops in international markets. This is especially evident in countries advocating localization to boost post-pandemic economic recovery. This is what makes it challenging for SBUX to bolster its growth in China.

SBUX Is Still Brewing Strength

Despite all these challenges, SBUX remains successful at dominating the global market. I know it’s not unbeatable, but it’s still very hard to beat at this point.

One factor to consider is its global scale. SBUX is almost ubiquitous, with 41,097 restaurants as of its Q3 2025 release. This contributes to its strong brand power and popularity. If you ask a random person to name a random coffee shop, I bet he or she will immediately say Starbucks. Here in the Philippines, Starbucks remains a go-to choice for many people across multiple income classes and households, whether employees or students.

Another factor is its strategic marketing strategy to ensure a solid customer base in its ecosystem. One marketing strategy is its Starbucks Rewards program, which has 75 million active members globally. This contributes 57% of its sales in the US. More interestingly, 43M members are from other countries, showing that its solid customer base is global. While there are no further surveys to support my claim, I believe that this is also effective in affecting or gaining consumer trust and affection. The sense of exclusivity is one aspect to consider. Meanwhile, the Endowment Effect leads consumers to value their ownership or membership very much. I know it’s easy to emulate. Tim Hortons also has this with its Tims Rewards. However, SBUX’s rewards program is still stickier due to its broader reward catalog, better customization, and larger global presence. With its current size and competitive advantage, I think it can afford to make more advanced gamifications and perks to stay in the lead. For instance, SBUX encourages customers by offering double-star days on certain days and birthdays.

The second marketing strategy is its atmosphere that fits a work and study setup. Newer branches have electrical outlets/sockets to allow consumers to work or study there for a few hours. This is to make these branches their second office or school. This is already common in the PH, and many coffee shops have done the same to gain traction. But it’s still Starbucks that remains a champion in this aspect. With that, it’s not surprising that many smaller coffee shop owners tend to build their own in underserved locations or those with less Starbucks presence.

Considering all these aspects, it’s safe to say that its business model remains strategic today. In fact, its Q3 2025 operating revenue reached $9.46B, up by 3.8% YoY from $9.11B. It’s also worth noting that its revenue growth in Q3 2025 is about twice its growth in nine months. This means that it increased much faster in the most recent quarter than in the first half of FY 2025. This is the second quarter with revenue growth after the YoY revenue decreases from Q2 2024 to Q1 2025. It also improved its efficiency as its operating leverage weakened from 85.6% to 85.3%. This means lower cost sensitivity, which is impressive amid stubborn inflation and continued store openings. Although it was not enough to manage the cost of new store openings, it helped SBUX maintain profitability. Its operating margin dropped from 15.8% to 9.5% YoY. But it was better than in Q2 2025 at 7.4%.

More interestingly, SBUX maintains a well-positioned and robust balance sheet to ensure its sustainability and dividend longevity. Its cash level has increased to more than $4B in the past 11 quarters. This should not be surprising since its operating cash flow has stayed enough to cover capex.

SBUX complements it with prudent debt management to maintain liquidity. What’s more interesting is that its maturity profile remains long enough to ensure timely repayments. Note that $8.9B or 51% of the total borrowings have a maturity of more than five years. This is in line with its Net Debt/EBITDA of only 2.4x, which means that the company only needs two years to repay all amount at once even if it doesn’t have to. Hence, SBUX has an adequate capital buffer to sustain its operations and dividends.

Valuation

SBUX has been moving with notable ups and downs in the past five years. But since 2023, the stock price has generally weakened. Despite this, I believe this can be an opportunity to buy it at a discount. While investors avoid it due to several issues I explained earlier, SBUX appears to be trading lower than necessary.

Using P/E, SBUX only trades at 36.61x its EPS versus its five-year average of 44.51x. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP will be $102.82. Now, you may say that the underpricing was due to 2020, which appears to be an outlier. So, I also used other financial metrics like sales per share and cash per share. Using P/S, the stock trades at only 2.62x its sales, the cheapest since 2020 and lower than the average of 3.56x. This suggests a TP of $115.15. Lastly, P/CPS shows that SBUX only trades at 22.92x versus the average of 28.33x. This is not the lowest ratio but close to the lower end of the data set. So, the TP is $104.54. To be more conservative, I also added a 10% risk consideration. But in the end, SBUX still appears cheap today.

To have a more forward-looking approach, I also used the Dividend Discount Model. For simplicity, I just used the annualized dividends of $2.44 and the dividend growth rate of 8.27%. Both are reported on Seeking Alpha. Meanwhile, I estimated the cost of equity using the capital asset pricing model. Note that I set its risk premium at 6.0%, which is higher than the standard of 5.0% to price in more risks. I computed its Beta on MS Excel by applying the SLOPE function to its daily price change versus the S&P 500. This is higher than the reported value of 0.98 on Yahoo Finance. To be more conservative, I used my estimated value. With all this information, I derived a TP of $114.55. And even after adding a 10% risk consideration, the TP is still high at $103.10. This is close to the P/S TP.

Technicals

SBUX’s technicals are bearish as the price continues to create lower highs and lows. The initial increase from April to July appeared to be a bull trap that led to a dip in the past two months. Now, the price is back below the 50-day and 200-day SMA. Meanwhile, the 50-day SMA is trending downward and also below the 200-day SMA, showing that market sentiments remain pessimistic. Yet, we can see that its low of $81 last September 12 did not break below the $76 support level, which shows that there is still a possibility of a rebound.

Its MACD and RSI are still consistent with the price trend, so we cannot expect a bullish divergence. However, the MACD line just crossed above the signal line and is now increasing, while the histogram is within positive territory again. This explains that selling volume is still high relative to buying volume, but the buying volume is starting to pick up again. This can also mean that the dip opened new entry opportunities to buyers. RSI is still normal, so there’s nothing special about it. The stock is no longer oversold. On the other hand, it is also far below the overbought region. So, there is still wide room for buying that can push the price up again.

With respect to Fibonacci Retracement, the price trend still appears quite sensitive to it. We can see how the price swings every time it touches a specific point. And recently, the dip crossed below the 0.236 point but did not touch 0.00 before the rebound. Hence, the price may rebound to the 0.382-0.50 range.

Conclusion

Starbucks Corporation sees various challenges that continue to hammer its growth potential. However, it stays competitive and formidable with its strategic business model, vast market coverage, and well-positioned Balance Sheet. Its valuation also appears attractive as it trades much lower than necessary. This is verified by its technicals as the recent dip did not break below the support level, while MACD and RSI show some buying opportunities. I am giving this stock a buy rating.
